source_paper_title: Asymptotically Optimal Control of Make-to-Stock Systems
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Gao_2024, title={Asymptotically Optimal Control of Make-to-Stock Systems}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2021.0161}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2021.0161}, number={2}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Gao, Xuefeng and Huang, Junfei}, year={2024}, month=may, pages={948–985} }
context:
Page 2, Introduction (early paragraphs).

Optimizing multi-product ATO systems is an immensely more difficult problem that remains unsolved.
title: Compute optimal policy for multi-product assemble-to-order systems with general lead times
background_latex:
An assemble-to-order (ATO) inventory system has:
1) A set of components indexed by \(j\in\{1,\dots,n\}\) and products indexed by \(i\in\{1,\dots,m\}\).
2) A bill-of-materials (BOM) matrix \(A\in\mathbb{Z}_+^{n\times m}\), where \(a_{ji}\) is the number of units of component \(j\) required to assemble one unit of product \(i\). Assume every row and every column of \(A\) has at least one positive entry.
3) Deterministic (possibly non-identical) component lead times \(L_j>0\). In continuous time, component \(j\) ordered at time \(t\) arrives at time \(t+L_j\).
4) Exogenous product demand arrivals over time, with unmet demand backlogged (no lost sales).

A control policy consists of:
1) Replenishment decisions specifying, over time, when and how many units of each component to order, subject to non-anticipativity.
2) Allocation/assembly decisions specifying how available components are assigned to fulfill backlogged product demands, subject to component availability implied by the BOM and replenishment arrivals.

Let \(I_j(t)\) be on-hand inventory of component \(j\) at time \(t\), and \(B_i(t)\) be backlog of product \(i\) at time \(t\). Holding costs \(h_j>0\) accrue per unit of component inventory per unit time, and backlog costs \(b_i>0\) accrue per unit of product backlog per unit time.

For a given feasible non-anticipating policy \(\pi\), define the long-run average expected total cost
\[
C(\pi)=\limsup_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T\left(\sum_{j=1}^n h_j\,\mathbb{E}[I_j(t)] + \sum_{i=1}^m b_i\,\mathbb{E}[B_i(t)]\right)\,dt.
\]
Let \(C^{\star}=\inf_{\pi} C(\pi)\) be the optimal value over all feasible policies.
problem_definition_latex: Determine an exactly optimal joint replenishment-and-allocation policy \(\pi^{\star}\) for a multi-product assemble-to-order inventory system with a general BOM matrix \(A\) and general deterministic (non-identical) component lead times \((L_j)_{j=1}^n\), under backlogging and long-run average expected holding-plus-backlog cost; equivalently, characterize \(C^{\star}=\inf_{\pi} C(\pi)\) and/or provide a policy attaining \(C^{\star}\).
latex:
An assemble-to-order (ATO) inventory system has:
1) A set of components indexed by \(j\in\{1,\dots,n\}\) and products indexed by \(i\in\{1,\dots,m\}\).
2) A bill-of-materials (BOM) matrix \(A\in\mathbb{Z}_+^{n\times m}\), where \(a_{ji}\) is the number of units of component \(j\) required to assemble one unit of product \(i\). Assume every row and every column of \(A\) has at least one positive entry.
3) Deterministic (possibly non-identical) component lead times \(L_j>0\). In continuous time, component \(j\) ordered at time \(t\) arrives at time \(t+L_j\).
4) Exogenous product demand arrivals over time, with unmet demand backlogged (no lost sales).

A control policy consists of:
1) Replenishment decisions specifying, over time, when and how many units of each component to order, subject to non-anticipativity.
2) Allocation/assembly decisions specifying how available components are assigned to fulfill backlogged product demands, subject to component availability implied by the BOM and replenishment arrivals.

Let \(I_j(t)\) be on-hand inventory of component \(j\) at time \(t\), and \(B_i(t)\) be backlog of product \(i\) at time \(t\). Holding costs \(h_j>0\) accrue per unit of component inventory per unit time, and backlog costs \(b_i>0\) accrue per unit of product backlog per unit time.

For a given feasible non-anticipating policy \(\pi\), define the long-run average expected total cost
\[
C(\pi)=\limsup_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T\left(\sum_{j=1}^n h_j\,\mathbb{E}[I_j(t)] + \sum_{i=1}^m b_i\,\mathbb{E}[B_i(t)]\right)\,dt.
\]
Let \(C^{\star}=\inf_{\pi} C(\pi)\) be the optimal value over all feasible policies.

Determine an exactly optimal joint replenishment-and-allocation policy \(\pi^{\star}\) for a multi-product assemble-to-order inventory system with a general BOM matrix \(A\) and general deterministic (non-identical) component lead times \((L_j)_{j=1}^n\), under backlogging and long-run average expected holding-plus-backlog cost; equivalently, characterize \(C^{\star}=\inf_{\pi} C(\pi)\) and/or provide a policy attaining \(C^{\star}\).
open_problem_quote: Optimizing multi-product ATO systems is an immensely more difficult problem that remains unsolved.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement is an existence/characterization problem for an optimal policy in a very broad stochastic control class, not a finite conjecture amenable to automated instance search. While one can compute optimal policies for small discretized models, that would not resolve the general unsolved optimal-control question.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary coding loop could search over parameterized policy classes and benchmark them via simulation/MDP approximation on families of instances, potentially uncovering structural conjectures. However, the open question is about exact optimality in general, and simulation-based fitness is only a heuristic proxy and unlikely to yield a definitive resolution.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open problem asks for an exactly optimal joint replenishment-and-allocation policy for a multi-product assemble-to-order system with a general BOM matrix \(A\) and general deterministic (non-identical) component lead times \((L_j)\), under backlogging and long-run average holding-plus-backlog cost. The 2021 paper by Reiman, Wan, and Wang frames this as a long-standing unsolved problem: the state must include on-hand inventories, backlogs, and the entire vector of outstanding orders across heterogeneous lead times, and replenishment and allocation are tightly coupled through the BOM constraints \(A Z(t)\le R(t-L)\).

Progress has largely come from (i) exact solutions in special cases and (ii) asymptotically optimal policies derived from stochastic-programming (SP) relaxations. Rosling (1989) gives an exact optimal policy for the single-product assembly case with multiple components and general lead times, illustrating that heterogenous lead times can be handled when allocation coupling is absent. For multi-product systems, Reiman and Wang (2012) introduce a multi-stage SP lower bound on the achievable average cost, and Reiman and Wang (2015) show that for identical lead times an SP-calibrated base-stock replenishment combined with an SP-guided allocation principle is asymptotically optimal as the common lead time grows.

The main advance toward the general-lead-time setting is Reiman, Wan, and Wang (2021), which replaces constant base-stock replenishment with dynamic inventory-position targets \(IP^k(t)\) that depend on demand observed over nested lead-time windows, and couples this with an allocation rule that tracks SP-implied backlog targets. A key technical contribution is a stochastic tracking model proving that actual inventory positions and backlogs track their targets on the diffusion scale, yielding \(\lim_{L\to\infty}(C^{(L)}-\underline C^{(L)})/\sqrt L=0\) and hence vanishing relative optimality gap as the longest lead time grows. However, this does not yield an exactly optimal policy for fixed finite lead times and general \(A\); the exact dynamic-programming characterization remains computationally and analytically intractable beyond special BOMs (e.g., N/W systems) where additional structural results are available (Lu, Song, and Zhang 2015). Remaining gaps include exact optimality for general BOM/lead times, tractable computation of the multi-stage SP (or its LP approximations) in large systems, and identifying broader structural classes where exact optimal policies (or finite-lead-time optimality of SP-based tracking policies) can be proved.

sources.title: Optimal inventory policies for assembly systems under random demands
sources.theorem_or_result: Derives exactly optimal control for single-product assembly systems with multiple components and general (non-identical) deterministic lead times under backlogging and linear holding/backlog costs; optimal policy can be expressed via coordinated inventory-position targets (a generalized base-stock structure).
sources.connection: Shows exact optimality is achievable in the single-product case and motivates why multi-product general BOM is harder: allocation coupling across products is absent when there is only one product.

sources.title: Asymptotically optimal inventory control for assemble-to-order systems with identical lead times
sources.theorem_or_result: For multi-product ATO with general BOM but identical component lead times, constructs a stochastic-programming (SP) lower bound and an SP-based replenishment/allocation policy that is asymptotically optimal as the common lead time grows (diffusion scale).
sources.connection: Provides a solved asymptotic version of the open problem in the special case of identical lead times; the 2021 paper generalizes this to non-identical lead times but still only asymptotically.

sources.title: A stochastic program based lower bound for assemble-to-order inventory systems
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces a multi-stage stochastic-programming relaxation that yields a computable lower bound on the long-run average cost for multi-product ATO systems; establishes the lower-bound property rigorously.
sources.connection: Supplies the key lower-bound/proxy model used in later asymptotic-optimality constructions; does not give an exactly optimal policy for general lead times.

sources.title: On the use of independent base-stock policies in assemble-to-order inventory systems with nonidentical lead times
sources.theorem_or_result: Shows regimes where simple independent base-stock replenishment (decoupled across components) is asymptotically optimal when lead-time differences are small relative to the lead times; provides performance guarantees and structural insight.
sources.connection: Addresses non-identical lead times but under a restricted asymptotic regime (near-identical lead times), not the fully general deterministic lead-time setting.

sources.title: A stochastic programming based inventory policy for assemble-to-order systems with application to the W model
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops an SP-based policy and analysis for the two-product W system (a canonical ATO with a common component), including numerical evidence and structural allocation insights; provides near-optimal performance relative to an SP lower bound.
sources.connection: Represents progress on special BOM structures (W system) but not a general exact solution; informs allocation heuristics and SP relaxations used in later general frameworks.

sources.title: Assemble-to-order inventory management via stochastic programming: Chained BOMs and the M-system
sources.theorem_or_result: Extends SP-based control ideas to chained BOM structures (M-system), characterizing allocation structure across cost regions and providing computational/numerical results.
sources.connection: Further partial progress on structured multi-product ATO; still not an exact general-lead-time solution but helps understand allocation coupling.

sources.title: Optimal and asymptotically optimal policies for assemble-to-order N-and W-systems
sources.theorem_or_result: For N and W systems, derives optimal or asymptotically optimal allocation/replenishment structures (including no-holdback-type rules) under continuous time, clarifying when simple priority allocation is optimal.
sources.connection: Gives exact/near-exact structural results for canonical small BOMs, illustrating what an exact solution can look like and why general BOMs are difficult.

sources.title: No-holdback allocation rules for continuous-time assemble-to-order systems
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies allocation rules (no-holdback) for continuous-time ATO systems, providing conditions under which greedy allocation is optimal or near-optimal and performance comparisons.
sources.connection: Informs the allocation side of the joint control problem; however does not resolve joint replenishment-allocation optimality for general BOM and lead times.

sources.title: Asymptotically Optimal Inventory Control for Assemble-to-Order Systems
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops a joint replenishment-and-allocation policy for multi-product ATO with general BOM and general deterministic non-identical lead times; proves diffusion-scale asymptotic optimality as the longest lead time grows using a multi-stage SP lower bound, dynamic inventory-position targets, and a stochastic tracking model.
sources.connection: Directly addresses the open problem but only in an asymptotic sense; does not provide an exactly optimal policy for finite lead times and general BOM.
